title: Bar and Rod: Forming Process and Centerline Discontinuities
narrationScript: Bar and rod are the ingot's story told in one dimension. Where rolling plate squeezes discontinuities flat, rolling and forging bar squeezes them into lines. A billet passes through grooved rolls, oval to round to oval, getting longer and thinner, and everything inside it gets drawn out along the axis. Pipe that survived cropping becomes a centerline thread of cavity. Inclusion clusters become stringers, like wires of foreign material running down the middle of the bar. Segregated metal becomes a segregated core. The geometry lesson is simple and powerful. In bar and rod, almost everything important is long, thin, and parallel to the axis, and it lives at or near the centerline. Review the forming route first. Ingots or continuously cast strands are rolled to blooms, blooms to billets, and billets to finished bar through a series of shaped passes. Large diameters may be forged instead, hammered or pressed between dies. Rod is rolled small and coiled, and much of it is later cold drawn through dies to make bright bar and wire, which improves the finish and the dimensions but adds its own stresses. Each step can add discontinuities of its own. Push the reduction too fast, or work the steel at the wrong temperature, and the center of the bar can rupture internally. We call those bursts, and unlike most bar discontinuities they are not neat axial planes. They are irregular, sometimes roughly transverse, and they live in the central zone. In drawn product, the die can set up a cycle of internal tension that cracks the core into a series of cup and cone shapes, called cupping or chevron cracking, again roughly transverse to the axis. Hydrogen in heavy sections can produce flakes, thin internal fissures in the mid radius zone with mostly random orientation. And the surface has its own family. Seams drawn out from billet defects, laps folded over by faulty passes, and cooling cracks. Now think like a beam of sound. Axial discontinuities at the center, pipe and stringers and segregation, present their long broadside to a beam fired radially through the diameter. So the bread and butter examination is a straight beam on the circumference, aimed at the center, with either the bar rotating under the probe or the probe indexing around and along the bar. A centerline reflector returns an echo from half the diameter, and the far wall echo drops. Run the numbers for a one hundred millimeter bar. The centerline sits fifty millimeters deep, and at five point nine two millimeters per microsecond for longitudinal waves in steel, that echo comes home in about seventeen microseconds round trip, with the far wall at about thirty four. Bursts, chevrons, and flakes punish a lazy technique. A roughly transverse crack at the center gives a weak answer to a radial beam, because the beam hits it edge on. You catch it with a straight beam from the bar end, where it sits broadside, or with an angle beam walking the sound along the axis from the outside surface. The round surface complicates coupling. A flat contact probe touches a convex bar along a line, not a face, and the curved entry surface spreads the beam in the metal, costing sensitivity at the center. Contoured shoes restore contact for smaller diameters, and high volume mills prefer immersion, where the water column couples perfectly and a focused probe can put the energy where the work is. Immersion also allows the offset technique. Shift the probe off the centerline by a calculated amount and refraction generates shear waves in the bar, which sweep the near surface region for seams and laps that a centered beam overlooks. Evaluation runs on reference reflectors. Bar and billet specifications typically set sensitivity with flat bottom holes drilled to specified depths, build a distance amplitude curve from them, and class the material by the largest acceptable response, with quality classes keyed to flat bottom hole sizes measured in sixty fourths of an inch. On top of that sit limits on loss of back reflection and on background noise, because a clean amplitude limit means nothing if grain noise is eating your margin. Surface methods often back up the ultrasound for seams and laps. The skill you are building in this lesson is orientation thinking. Before you scan a bar, picture the discontinuities the process plants in it, aim your beam to hit them broadside, and choose a second direction for the ones the first scan cannot see.
imagePrompt: An inspector holds a contoured ultrasonic probe against a slowly rotating steel bar supported on rollers in a mill bay, couplant glistening on the bar and racks of round stock in the background.

sections.heading: From Billet to Bar: One-Dimensional Squeezing
sections.body:
Bar and rod come from the same casting stock as plate, worked in a different shape. Ingots or continuously cast strands are rolled to **blooms**, blooms to **billets**, and billets to finished bar through a series of shaped roll passes — oval to round to oval — each one making the product longer and thinner. Very large diameters are **forged** between dies instead. **Rod** is rolled small and coiled; much of it is later **cold drawn** through dies into bright bar and wire for finish and dimensional control, a step that adds residual stress of its own.

All of that working deforms the metal in one dominant direction: **along the axis**. The consequences mirror what rolling did to plate, rotated into a cylinder:

- Surviving **pipe** becomes a thread of cavity along the centerline.
- **Inclusion clusters** become **stringers** — wires of nonmetallic material running lengthwise at and near the center.
- **Centerline segregation** becomes a chemically dirty core.

Higher reduction ratios stretch these features longer and thinner and refine the grain — good for properties, and good for UT, because wrought structure passes sound far better than the as-cast bloom did.

sections.caption: Longitudinal section of hot-rolled bar showing centerline pipe, stringers, bursts, chevron cracking, and surface seams, with the radial, end, and angle-beam scan directions that catch each.

sections.heading: The Discontinuity Cast: Centerline, Core, and Surface
sections.body:
Inherited from the ingot, running **axially at or near the centerline**: **pipe** remnants, **stringers**, and **segregation**. These are the classic bar discontinuities, and their orientation is beautifully predictable.

Added by the forming work itself:

- **Bursts** — internal ruptures from excessive reduction per pass or working at improper temperature. They live in the central zone but are **irregular**, often roughly transverse or randomly oriented — the troublemakers of bar UT.
- **Cupping / chevron cracking** — in cold-drawn product, die stresses can crack the core into a periodic series of cup-and-cone or arrowhead separations, **transverse to the axis**.
- **Flakes** — thin internal fissures from dissolved hydrogen in heavy sections of medium- and high-carbon or alloy steel, typically in the **mid-radius zone** with near-random orientation.

On the surface: **seams** (rolled-out billet or casting surface defects, long and axial), **laps** (fins folded over by over-filled passes, sometimes slightly spiral), and **cooling cracks**. Surface discontinuities are commonly confirmed with magnetic particle or eddy current methods, but they matter to UT too — they generate noise, kill coupling locally, and can mask what is underneath.

sections.caption: Bar and rod discontinuities: origin, orientation, and the beam that finds them.
rows: Radial straight beam — strikes it broadside
Radial straight beam; expect strings of small echoes
Excessive reduction or improper working temperature
Central zone; irregular, often roughly transverse
Radial scan plus an axial or angle-beam supplement
Straight beam from the bar end, or angle beam along the axis
Radial scan; drop frequency if grain noise allows no margin
Axial or slightly spiral, at the surface
Offset-immersion shear technique; confirm with MT/ET

sections.heading: Radial Scanning and What the Screen Shows
sections.body:
The bread-and-butter examination is a **straight beam on the circumference aimed at the center**, with the bar rotating under the probe or the probe indexed in a helix — coverage requires the scan pitch to overlap the effective beam width. On the screen, sound bar shows the initial pulse and a far-wall echo at one diameter. A **centerline reflector** announces itself as an echo at **half the diameter** with a weakened far-wall echo behind it.

Put numbers on a 100 mm bar with longitudinal steel velocity 5.92 mm/µs: the centerline echo returns from 50 mm in about **16.9 µs round trip**, the far wall from 100 mm at about 33.8 µs. Stringers paint repeating small echoes at the same depth as the bar rotates; pipe gives a stronger, more continuous response along the length.

Two cautions. First, the radial beam **converges on the center from every angle of rotation**, so centerline flaws get maximum looks — but an off-axis flaw is only hit broadside twice per revolution, which is why index overlap matters. Second, large alloy bar can still carry coarse grain: if grass erodes your margin, drop from 5 MHz to 2.25 MHz and re-verify the reference. Near-surface coverage on small diameters needs a delay line, dual-element probe, or immersion, because the dead zone eats the first few millimeters.

sections.caption: Radial scanning geometry on 100 mm bar: a centerline reflector echoes from 50 mm depth and weakens the far-wall echo.

sections.title: Hit it broadside — or scan a second direction
sections.body: Radial beams see axial discontinuities beautifully and **transverse ones hardly at all**. Bursts and chevron cracks answer a radial beam edge-on with almost nothing. When the specification covers transverse discontinuities, add a **straight beam from the bar end** or an **angle beam directed along the axis** — never sign off bar stock on one beam direction alone.

sections.heading: Coupling on Curves; Standards and Classes
sections.body:
A flat contact probe meets a convex bar along a **line**, not a face: contact area shrinks, the probe rocks, and the curved entry surface spreads the beam in the metal, costing sensitivity at the center. **Contoured shoes** matched to the diameter restore stable coupling for manual work. High-volume testing prefers **immersion**: the water column couples uniformly, focused probes concentrate energy where the evaluation zone is, and rotating-bar machines scan helically at production speed.

Immersion adds the **offset technique**: displace the probe axis a calculated distance off the bar centerline and refraction converts the beam to **shear waves** sweeping the near-surface zone — the standard trick for seams and laps that a centered beam overlooks.

Evaluation follows wrought-product specifications of the ASTM E2375 / AMS-STD-2154 pattern or forging practices like ASTM A388, invoked by the purchase order:

- Sensitivity from **flat-bottom-hole** reference standards, with a **distance-amplitude curve** spanning the evaluation depth.
- **Quality classes** keyed to FBH size in 1/64 in. steps (a 3/64 in. class is tighter than 5/64 in.), with limits on single and multiple indications.
- Supplementary limits on **loss of back reflection** and on **noise level**, protecting the margin your amplitude criteria assume.

Record axial position, circumferential position, depth, and amplitude versus the DAC — and note which beam direction found each indication.

figures.title: Bar stock discontinuities and scan directions

checkQuestions.question: Why is a radial straight beam the primary scan for bar and rod?
options: It avoids mode conversion at the curved surface
The dominant discontinuities — pipe, stringers, segregation — run axially at the center, so a radial beam strikes them broadside
Radial beams are immune to grain-scattering noise
The far-wall echo is always strongest in the radial direction
checkQuestions.explanation: Forming elongates ingot discontinuities along the bar axis at and near the centerline; a beam fired through the diameter meets those long reflectors at normal incidence, the orientation that returns maximum energy.

checkQuestions.question: An 80 mm steel bar is scanned radially (longitudinal velocity 5.92 mm/µs). About when does an echo from a centerline reflector arrive, round trip?
options: About 6.8 µs
About 13.5 µs
About 27.0 µs
About 16.9 µs
checkQuestions.explanation: The centerline sits 40 mm deep, so the round trip is 80 mm: 80 ÷ 5.92 ≈ 13.5 µs. The far wall at 80 mm depth would arrive at about 27 µs.

checkQuestions.question: Chevron (cupping) cracks in cold-drawn bar are best detected with which approach, and why?
options: Radial straight beam, because they sit on the centerline
A straight beam from the bar end or an angle beam along the axis, because the cracks lie transverse to the bar axis
Surface waves, because cupping starts at the surface
Through-transmission across the diameter, because cupping blocks all sound
checkQuestions.explanation: Chevron cracks are roughly transverse separations on the axis; a radial beam meets them edge-on and gets almost nothing back, while an end scan or axially directed angle beam hits them broadside.

checkQuestions.question: Why do high-volume bar mills prefer immersion testing over flat contact probes?
options: Water slows the beam, improving resolution
A flat probe meets the convex bar along a line and the curved surface spreads the beam, while immersion couples uniformly and allows focused probes and offset shear-wave techniques
Immersion eliminates the need for reference standards
Contact testing cannot generate longitudinal waves in round stock
checkQuestions.explanation: Curvature ruins flat-probe contact and defocuses the beam; immersion restores uniform coupling, lets focused probes concentrate energy, and supports the offset technique for near-surface seams.

checkQuestions.question: A bar specification assigns quality classes keyed to flat-bottom-hole sizes. What does a tighter class fundamentally change?
options: The bar diameter that may be tested
The maximum acceptable indication amplitude, referenced to a smaller FBH on the distance-amplitude curve
The couplant required for scanning
The number of back-wall multiples that must be visible
checkQuestions.explanation: Classes set the rejection threshold: a 3/64 in. FBH class rejects smaller responses than a 5/64 in. class, with the DAC carrying that threshold across the evaluation depth — usually alongside back-reflection-loss and noise limits.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §2.3 (bar and rod: forming, discontinuities, response, codes/standards)
Wrought-product practices such as ASTM E2375, AMS-STD-2154, and ASTM A388 with FBH/DAC acceptance classes (cited generically)
Hot-rolling, forging, and cold-drawing of long products — general references
